---
title: "hecfda"
toc: false
---

hecfda is an (unofficial) C++17 port of the numerical core of
[HEC-FDA](https://github.com/HydrologicEngineeringCenter/HEC-FDA), the flood damage-reduction
analysis tool from the USACE
[Hydrologic Engineering Center](https://github.com/HydrologicEngineeringCenter) (HEC). One shared
C++ core is bound into an R package (`hecfdar`) and a Python package (`hecfdapy`), so a
stage-frequency curve, a structure inventory, and a seeded Monte Carlo simulation all produce
identical results in either language, matching the upstream C#.

The full pipeline is ported: stage-damage curves from a structure inventory, the seeded Monte
Carlo expected annual damage (EAD) simulation, scenarios across impact areas, equivalent annual
damage (EqAD) annualization, and with/without-project benefits.

See the [porting status page](status.qmd) for what is implemented and what is deliberately out of
scope (GIS, terrain, persistence, and UI layers are not part of this port).

## Quick start

Both packages run the same seeded Monte Carlo engine, so the deterministic HEC-FDA oracle value
150000 comes back identically in R and Python.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Python

```python
import hecfdapy as fda

res = fda.ead_simulation(
    stage_damage=[{
        "x": [0, 15, 30], "dist": "Uniform",
        "params": [[0, 0, 10], [0, 600000, 10], [0, 600000, 10]],
        "damage_category": "residential", "asset_category": "Structure",
    }],
    flow_frequency={"dist": "Uniform", "params": [0, 100000, 1000]},
    flow_stage={
        "x": [0, 100000], "dist": "Uniform",
        "params": [[0, 0, 10], [0, 30, 10]],
    },
    min_iterations=1, max_iterations=1, deterministic=True,
)
res["total_ead"]  # 150000
```

## R

```r
library(hecfdar)

res = ead_simulation(
  stage_damage = list(list(
    x = c(0, 15, 30), dist = "Uniform",
    params = list(c(0, 0, 10), c(0, 600000, 10), c(0, 600000, 10)),
    damage_category = "residential", asset_category = "Structure"
  )),
  flow_frequency = list(dist = "Uniform", params = c(0, 100000, 1000)),
  flow_stage = list(
    x = c(0, 100000), dist = "Uniform",
    params = list(c(0, 0, 10), c(0, 30, 10))
  ),
  min_iterations = 1L, max_iterations = 1L, deterministic = TRUE
)
res$total_ead  # 150000
```

:::

## Install

Both packages are pre-release and install directly from GitHub. They compile the shared core
from source, so a C++17 compiler is required (Xcode command-line tools on macOS, gcc/clang on
Linux, Rtools on Windows for R).

```bash
pip install "git+https://github.com/cameronbracken/hecfda.git#subdirectory=hecfdapy"
```

```r
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("cameronbracken/hecfda/hecfdar")
```

Releases on PyPI and CRAN are planned; these instructions will switch to plain `pip install` /
`install.packages()` when they land.

## Learn more

- [Worked examples](examples/index.qmd), each shown in both Python and R and checked against the
  upstream C# outputs.
- [Python API reference](reference/index.qmd)
- [R API reference](r/index.html)
